title: 10_frozen_backbone.ipynb
descriptionMd:
## Mission: Bring Up the PCB Defect Classifier on 1,200 Photos

Quality inspection has 1,200 labeled photos of circuit boards spanning four defect classes — far too few to train a CNN from scratch. The plan is textbook transfer learning: take an ImageNet-pretrained ResNet-18, keep its feature extractor frozen so the tiny dataset cannot wreck it, swap the 1000-class head for a fresh 4-way linear layer, and train only that head for a few epochs.

The very first smoke run died deep inside `loss.backward()` with a cryptic autograd error about tensors that do not require grad. A teammate added the trainable-parameter audit above the training loop to make the failure legible, and now it fails fast — with a brutal number: zero. The head you just attached was supposed to be the one part of this network that learns. Figure out where its gradients went.
initialCode:
import torch
from torch import nn
from torchvision import models

from defects.data import train_loader  # 1,200 board photos, 4 defect classes

model = models.resnet18(weights="IMAGENET1K_V1")
model.fc = nn.Linear(model.fc.in_features, 4)

for param in model.parameters():
    param.requires_grad = False

optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=1e-3)
loss_fn = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()

trainable = sum(p.numel() for p in model.parameters() if p.requires_grad)
print(f"trainable params: {trainable:,}")
assert trainable > 0, f"nothing will train: {trainable} trainable params"

for epoch in range(3):
    running = 0.0
    for images, labels in train_loader:
        optimizer.zero_grad()
        loss = loss_fn(model(images), labels)
        loss.backward()
        optimizer.step()
        running += loss.item()
    print(f"epoch {epoch}: loss {running / len(train_loader):.4f}")
traceback:

---------------------------------------------------------------------------

---> 18 assert trainable > 0, f"nothing will train: {trainable} trainable params"

AssertionError: nothing will train: 0 trainable params
correctOutput:

epoch 1: loss 0.5117

options.label: Re-enable the head after the freeze with model.fc.requires_grad = True
options.patchCode:
import torch
from torch import nn
from torchvision import models

from defects.data import train_loader  # 1,200 board photos, 4 defect classes

model = models.resnet18(weights="IMAGENET1K_V1")
model.fc = nn.Linear(model.fc.in_features, 4)

for param in model.parameters():
    param.requires_grad = False
model.fc.requires_grad = True

optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=1e-3)
loss_fn = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()

trainable = sum(p.numel() for p in model.parameters() if p.requires_grad)
print(f"trainable params: {trainable:,}")
assert trainable > 0, f"nothing will train: {trainable} trainable params"

for epoch in range(3):
    running = 0.0
    for images, labels in train_loader:
        optimizer.zero_grad()
        loss = loss_fn(model(images), labels)
        loss.backward()
        optimizer.step()
        running += loss.item()
    print(f"epoch {epoch}: loss {running / len(train_loader):.4f}")
options.resultLog:
trainable params: 0
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
AssertionError                            Traceback (most recent call last)
Cell In[1], line 19
     17 trainable = sum(p.numel() for p in model.parameters() if p.requires_grad)
     18 print(f"trainable params: {trainable:,}")
---> 19 assert trainable > 0, f"nothing will train: {trainable} trainable params"

AssertionError: nothing will train: 0 trainable params
options.rationale: `model.fc` is an `nn.Module`, not a tensor. `requires_grad` lives on parameter tensors, and `nn.Module.__setattr__` only intercepts Parameters, Modules, and buffers — a plain boolean falls through and becomes an inert Python attribute. No error, no warning, no parameter touched: the count stays 0 and the identical assert fires. The spellings that actually reach the tensors are `model.fc.requires_grad_(True)` or a loop over `model.fc.parameters()` — but even those are a patch on top of a sweep that should never have caught the head in the first place.

options.label: Delete the freeze loop — let the whole network train
options.patchCode:
import torch
from torch import nn
from torchvision import models

from defects.data import train_loader  # 1,200 board photos, 4 defect classes

model = models.resnet18(weights="IMAGENET1K_V1")
model.fc = nn.Linear(model.fc.in_features, 4)

optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=1e-3)
loss_fn = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()

trainable = sum(p.numel() for p in model.parameters() if p.requires_grad)
print(f"trainable params: {trainable:,}")
assert trainable > 0, f"nothing will train: {trainable} trainable params"

for epoch in range(3):
    running = 0.0
    for images, labels in train_loader:
        optimizer.zero_grad()
        loss = loss_fn(model(images), labels)
        loss.backward()
        optimizer.step()
        running += loss.item()
    print(f"epoch {epoch}: loss {running / len(train_loader):.4f}")
options.resultLog:
trainable params: 11,178,564
epoch 0: loss 1.8473
epoch 1: loss 0.6911
epoch 2: loss 0.2104
options.rationale: The assert goes green — 11,178,564 trainable parameters — and the final train loss even beats the frozen-backbone run, which is exactly the trap. Adam at 1e-3 is now rewriting every pretrained filter using 1,200 photos: epoch 0 is *worse* than random-head territory because the early oversized updates damage the ImageNet features, and the recovery afterwards is the network memorizing the training set. Validation accuracy will crater, and the job now pays full-backbone gradient memory and compute. The mission was a frozen backbone; a passing assert was never the goal by itself.

options.label: Freeze the backbone first, then attach the head, and give Adam only trainable params
options.patchCode:
import torch
from torch import nn
from torchvision import models

from defects.data import train_loader  # 1,200 board photos, 4 defect classes

model = models.resnet18(weights="IMAGENET1K_V1")

for param in model.parameters():
    param.requires_grad = False

model.fc = nn.Linear(model.fc.in_features, 4)

optimizer = torch.optim.Adam([p for p in model.parameters() if p.requires_grad], lr=1e-3)
loss_fn = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()

trainable = sum(p.numel() for p in model.parameters() if p.requires_grad)
print(f"trainable params: {trainable:,}")
assert trainable > 0, f"nothing will train: {trainable} trainable params"

for epoch in range(3):
    running = 0.0
    for images, labels in train_loader:
        optimizer.zero_grad()
        loss = loss_fn(model(images), labels)
        loss.backward()
        optimizer.step()
        running += loss.item()
    print(f"epoch {epoch}: loss {running / len(train_loader):.4f}")
options.resultLog:
trainable params: 2,052
epoch 0: loss 1.0231
epoch 1: loss 0.5117
epoch 2: loss 0.3408
options.rationale: Order is the whole bug: `model.parameters()` yields whatever parameters exist *at the moment the loop runs*. Freezing first means the sweep can only touch the pretrained backbone; the head attached afterwards is born with `requires_grad=True`, like every fresh layer. Filtering the optimizer to trainable parameters encodes the same contract a second time — had the original ordering survived, Adam would have failed loudly at construction with `ValueError: optimizer got an empty parameter list` instead of silently holding frozen tensors. The audit now reads 2,052: the head's weight and bias, and nothing else.

hints: A blanket `requires_grad=False` over `model.parameters()` freezes everything the model owns *at that instant* — there is no built-in notion of 'backbone only'. Whether a layer escapes the sweep depends entirely on whether it exists yet when the sweep runs.

Lines 10–11: this freeze loop runs after line 8 already swapped in the new `fc`, so the head's weight and bias are inside `model.parameters()` and get swept up with the backbone. Freshly constructed layers default to `requires_grad=True` — the question is what gets created before versus after the freeze.
explanationMd:
### Why the bug occurs
`model.parameters()` is a live view of the module tree as it exists right now, not a description of the architecture in the author's head. Line 8 replaces the 1000-class head with a fresh 4-way `nn.Linear`, whose weight and bias — like every newly constructed parameter — start out with `requires_grad=True`. Lines 10–11 then sweep `requires_grad=False` across every parameter the model owns, and at that instant the new head is already in the tree. Nothing in the loop distinguishes backbone from head; that distinction existed only as intent. The result is a network with zero trainable parameters: the audit prints 0 and the assert fires. Without the assert it would be worse — the first `loss.backward()` raises `RuntimeError: element 0 of tensors does not require grad and does not have a grad_fn`, far from the line that caused it.

### Why the fix is correct
Freeze first, attach second. With the order swapped, the blanket loop can only ever touch parameters that exist at that point — the pretrained backbone — and the head created afterwards keeps its trainable default. Passing `[p for p in model.parameters() if p.requires_grad]` to Adam encodes the intent at a second gate: if a future refactor ever freezes everything again, the optimizer fails at construction with `ValueError: optimizer got an empty parameter list` instead of training nothing. The print-and-assert pins the invariant to a number — 2,052 head parameters — so a regression breaks the build, not an overnight run.
recruiterReview: Exactly right — and I like that you fixed the *order* instead of bolting an unfreeze on top of the broken sweep. The underlying principle is that `model.parameters()` is a live iterator, so mutation order against it is part of the program's correctness, not style. Filtering the optimizer to trainable parameters is what separates this from a lucky guess: it turns the next freezing mistake into a loud `ValueError` at construction time rather than a silent non-training run. For the follow-up PR, log the frozen and trainable counts side by side at startup — when we unfreeze `layer4` for stage-two fine-tuning, that audit will catch a half-frozen optimizer before it burns a GPU-night. Approved. ✅

tutorial.bodyMd:
## Freezing Parameters Without Freezing Your Model

Transfer learning rests on a quiet division of labor: a pretrained backbone supplies general-purpose features, and a small fresh head learns your task. The mechanism that enforces the division is a single boolean on every parameter tensor — `requires_grad` — and almost every freezing bug comes from forgetting two facts about it: it is *per-tensor state*, and it is evaluated *at the moment you set it*.

### requires_grad is state on tensors, not a property of modules
There is no frozen flag on `nn.Module`. When you "freeze a layer" you are looping over its parameter tensors and flipping each one's `requires_grad` to `False`. Three consequences are worth memorizing. First, autograd stops computing gradients for those tensors, so the optimizer has nothing to apply. Second, a module is just a container: writing `module.requires_grad = True` creates an inert Python attribute and touches no parameter — the real spellings are `module.requires_grad_(True)` or a loop over `module.parameters()`. Third, every freshly constructed layer is born trainable: `requires_grad=True` is the default for new parameters.

### Mutation order is part of the API
`model.parameters()` is a live view of the module tree, not a snapshot of the architecture you intend. A blanket freeze sweeps everything that exists at that instant; it cannot honor intentions like "backbone only". So sequence the surgery deliberately: **freeze first, then attach new parts**. Anything created after the sweep keeps its trainable default, which means the order of two adjacent lines decides whether your head learns or your whole run is theater.

### The optimizer is a second, independent gate
A parameter only updates if (a) autograd writes a gradient into it and (b) some optimizer holds a reference to it. Freezing controls the first gate; the optimizer's param list controls the second. Passing `[p for p in model.parameters() if p.requires_grad]` keeps the gates aligned — and adds a free tripwire, because PyTorch raises `ValueError: optimizer got an empty parameter list` if a freeze accidentally caught everything. The reverse mismatch is sneakier: an optimizer constructed *before* you swapped a layer still holds the orphaned old tensors, gradients flow into the new layer but nothing ever steps it, and the loss sits perfectly flat with no error at all.

### Audit, then assert
Freezing failures rarely crash; they impersonate bad hyperparameters. Make the invisible state visible: count trainable parameters and assert the expected number before the first epoch. Counts beat names because they catch a half-frozen layer, not just a missing one. Learn the signatures: a flat loss curve means something that should train is frozen or unreferenced; `RuntimeError: element 0 of tensors does not require grad` means everything is frozen; a suspiciously good train loss with terrible validation means nothing is frozen and a small dataset is overwriting the pretrained features.

### Common variations of this bug
- Blanket-freezing after the new head is attached, so the head gets swept up too (this challenge).
- Writing `module.requires_grad = True/False` on an `nn.Module` — a silent no-op attribute instead of `requires_grad_()`.
- Replacing a layer *after* constructing the optimizer, leaving the optimizer stepping orphaned old tensors: flat loss, no error.
- Confusing `model.eval()` with freezing — eval mode changes dropout and batch-norm behavior, but every parameter still trains.
- Unfreezing layers for stage-two fine-tuning but never adding them to the optimizer (or a new param group), so they still never move.
- Assuming `requires_grad` flags travel inside checkpoints — `load_state_dict` restores values, not freeze state.
